title: Creating and Connecting to Your First Linux Server
description: Spin up an Ubuntu 24.04 Linux server in the cloud, configure SSH key authentication, and learn to navigate your new server. This exercise uses DigitalOcean but the steps work with any cloud provider.
prerequisites: A computer with a terminal (macOS, Linux, or Windows with WSL)
A cloud server (we use DigitalOcean in this exercise, but any Ubuntu 24.04 server works)
Basic familiarity with typing commands in a terminal
learningObjectives: Create a cloud Linux server running Ubuntu 24.04
Generate and configure SSH keys for secure access
Connect to a remote server via SSH
Navigate the Linux filesystem and run basic commands
Understand the difference between root and regular user access

sponsorCta.text: You need a Linux server to follow along. Any cloud provider works, but we recommend DigitalOcean for its simple interface and fast setup.

steps.title: Generate an SSH Key Pair
steps.description: Before creating a server, generate an SSH key pair on your local machine. This lets you log in securely without a password.
hints: Press Enter to accept the default file location (~/.ssh/id_ed25519)
You can set a passphrase for extra security or leave it empty
If you already have an SSH key, you can skip generation and just copy your public key
validationCriteria: The file ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 exists
The file ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub exists
You can see your public key output

steps.title: Create an Ubuntu 24.04 Droplet
steps.description: Log in to DigitalOcean, add your SSH public key, and create a new droplet. Choose Ubuntu 24.04 LTS as the image. A Basic plan with 1 GB RAM and 1 vCPU ($6/month) is enough for learning. Pick the datacenter region closest to you.
steps.expectedOutput: Copy this output and paste it into the DigitalOcean SSH key form during droplet creation.
hints: Go to cloud.digitalocean.com and click 'Create' then 'Droplets'
Select Ubuntu 24.04 (LTS) x64 as your image
Choose the $6/month Basic plan for testing
Under Authentication, select 'SSH keys' and add your public key
Give your droplet a hostname like 'ubuntu-lab'
validationCriteria: Droplet is created and shows a 'Running' status in the DigitalOcean dashboard
The droplet has a public IPv4 address assigned
Your SSH key is listed under the droplet's access settings

steps.title: Connect to Your Server via SSH
steps.description: Use SSH to connect to your new droplet. Replace the IP address below with the one shown in your DigitalOcean dashboard.
steps.expectedOutput:
Welcome to Ubuntu 24.04 LTS (GNU/Linux 6.8.0-xx-generic x86_64)

 * Documentation:  https://help.ubuntu.com
 * Management:     https://landscape.canonical.com
 * Support:        https://ubuntu.com/pro

root@ubuntu-lab:~#
hints: Replace YOUR_DROPLET_IP with the actual IP from your dashboard
Type 'yes' when asked to confirm the host fingerprint on first connection
If connection times out, verify the droplet is running and your IP is correct
If you get 'Permission denied', double check that you added the correct SSH public key
validationCriteria: You see the Ubuntu welcome message
Your prompt shows root@your-hostname
You are logged in as root

steps.title: Explore Your Server
steps.description: Run some basic commands to understand what you are working with. Check the OS version, available resources, and disk space.
steps.expectedOutput:
PRETTY_NAME="Ubuntu 24.04 LTS"
NAME="Ubuntu"
VERSION_ID="24.04"
...

Linux ubuntu-lab 6.8.0-xx-generic #xx-Ubuntu SMP x86_64 GNU/Linux

               total        used        free
Mem:           976Mi       150Mi       500Mi

Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/vda1        25G  2.1G   22G   9% /

1
hints: free -h shows memory in human-readable format (MB, GB)
df -h shows disk usage for all mounted filesystems
nproc shows the number of CPU cores available
validationCriteria: OS version shows Ubuntu 24.04
Memory and disk info are displayed
CPU count matches your droplet plan

steps.title: Navigate the Linux Filesystem
steps.description: Learn to move around the filesystem, list files, and understand the directory structure of a Linux server.
steps.expectedOutput:
/root

total 68
drwxr-xr-x  19 root root  4096 ... .
drwxr-xr-x  19 root root  4096 ... ..
drwxr-xr-x   2 root root  4096 ... bin
drwxr-xr-x   3 root root  4096 ... boot
drwxr-xr-x   5 root root   380 ... dev
drwxr-xr-x  92 root root  4096 ... etc
...

/tmp
/root
hints: pwd prints your current working directory
ls -la shows all files including hidden ones, with permissions and ownership
cd ~ always takes you back to your home directory
The root user's home directory is /root, not /home/root
validationCriteria: You can navigate between directories
You understand the top-level directory layout
You can return to your home directory with cd ~

steps.title: Update the System Packages
steps.description: Always update your server packages right after creating it. This ensures you have the latest security patches.
steps.expectedOutput:
Hit:1 http://mirrors.digitalocean.com/ubuntu noble InRelease
Get:2 http://mirrors.digitalocean.com/ubuntu noble-updates InRelease [126 kB]
...
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
All packages are up to date.

OR

The following packages will be upgraded:
  ...
X upgraded, 0 newly installed, 0 to remove and 0 not upgraded.
hints: apt update refreshes the list of available packages
apt upgrade actually installs the updates
The -y flag automatically answers 'yes' to prompts
Run both commands every time you start working on a fresh server
validationCriteria: apt update completes without errors
apt upgrade installs any available updates
No errors in the output

steps.title: Install Essential Tools
steps.description: Install a few commonly used tools that are not included by default. These will be useful throughout the rest of this series.
commands: apt install -y curl wget vim htop unzip git
steps.expectedOutput:
Reading package lists... Done
...
The following NEW packages will be installed:
  curl git htop unzip vim wget
...

htop 3.3.0
git version 2.43.0
curl 8.5.0 (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)
hints: You can install multiple packages in a single apt install command
These tools are useful for downloading files, editing configs, monitoring, and version control
htop is an interactive process viewer, much better than plain top
validationCriteria: All packages install without errors
htop, git, and curl are available and show version output
You can run htop to see the process viewer (press q to quit)

completionCriteria: You have a running Ubuntu 24.04 droplet on DigitalOcean
You can connect via SSH using your key pair
You can navigate the filesystem and run basic commands

resources.title: How to Set Up SSH Keys on Ubuntu

troubleshooting.issue: SSH connection refused or times out
troubleshooting.solution: Verify the droplet is running in the DigitalOcean dashboard. Check that you are using the correct IP address. If you just created the droplet, wait 30 seconds for it to finish booting.

troubleshooting.issue: Permission denied (publickey) when connecting via SSH
troubleshooting.solution: Make sure you added the correct public key (id_ed25519.pub, not the private key) to DigitalOcean before creating the droplet. You can also add it via the dashboard under Settings > Security > SSH Keys.

troubleshooting.issue: apt update fails with repository errors
troubleshooting.solution: This can happen if DigitalOcean mirrors are temporarily slow. Wait a minute and try again. If the problem persists, check your server's internet connectivity with ping 8.8.8.8.
